_about: PHALANX Pass-2 (SPARTA) mappings, authored per ENISA threat. Pass-1 fields (L, S, A, TB, E) are derived by the engine from the affected-asset set and are NOT stored here. Each entry authors Pre, TTP, CM, I, Conf by applying decision table R1-R10. Every SPARTA id is verified against framework/webapp/src/data/sparta.json; every claim traces to a primary source (ENISA STL, SPARTA v3.2, CCSDS, NIST). TR52 is the calibrated worked example / template.

_schema.pre: string - enabling precondition(s); the state->action bridge (R3)
_schema.ttp: [{id, name, tactic, kind, note}] - SPARTA technique chain in attacker order. kind is one of entry|bridge|effect|step and drives the R9 bridge marker (explicit, never inferred from the note text); note is the analyst's per-step reasoning, rendered under the chain (R1 map, R6 many-to-many, R9 bridging sub-chain when the record crosses a segment boundary)
_schema.cm_sparta: [{id, name, stage}] - each CM is mapped in sparta.json to the stage technique it is listed under (R8: bind CM to a specific stage, not the threat category). Do NOT list a CM that sparta.json does not map to a technique in this chain.
_schema.cm_standards: [string] - standards crosswalk overlay (CCSDS / NIST SP 800-53 families). Scope each correctly.
_schema.cm_enisa: string - ENISA threat-level controls are auto-ranked by the tool (enisa_controls.json, ISO/NIST/NIS2/CCSDS-traceable); dual-sourced with the SPARTA CMs. Not hand-entered here.
_schema.impact: string - technical & mission impact; ties to the SPARTA Impact tactic + the ENISA CIA tag
_schema.conf: {grade, note} - one record-level STIX 2.1 grade High/Med/Low (R7), taken at the weakest link; note explains what is sourced vs inferred.

TR52.name: Seizure of control: Satellite bus
TR52.pre: The ground / management segment has an exploitable, reachable vulnerability (enables the takeover), AND the on-board C&DH accepts telecommands from the ground segment without independent anomaly / rate control. Once the ground system is compromised, the bus executes attacker-issued *valid* (legitimately authenticated) telecommands and can be driven to denial / operator lockout. Note: the related link-injection variant (forged / replayed commands) additionally requires the TC link to lack SDLS authentication / integrity - that variant is a distinct record, not this one.

ttp.name: Compromise Ground System
ttp.note: The way in, at the Ground / management segment. ENISA states the takeover proceeds by "overtaking control of the management segment" - so the ground system, not the link, is the entry point.

ttp.name: Valid Commands
ttp.note: Crosses the Ground <-> Space telecommand-link trust boundary (R9). Because the ground system is already compromised, the telecommands it issues are legitimately authenticated - they are *valid* commands, which is why link authentication alone does not stop this step. Lateral movement then carries the attacker over the command link into the satellite bus.

ttp.name: Disruption
ttp.note: Disruption of the satellite bus in the Space segment.

ttp.name: Denial
ttp.note: Denial of platform control - complete lockout of legitimate operators. Matches the ENISA CIA tag for this threat (Availability).

cm_standards: NIST SP 800-53: AC (access control) + IA (identification & authentication) - harden the ground/management segment against IA-0007
NIST SP 800-53: SI (system & information integrity) + SC (system & communications protection) - on-board detection & command-path protection
NIST SP 800-53: CP (contingency planning) - availability recovery (maps to CM0070)

CCSDS 355.0-B-2 (SDLS) + 355.1-B-1 (SDLS-EP: key management / OTAR) - authenticate & integrity-protect the TC link. NOTE: this primarily defends the forged/replayed-command variant (EX-0014 / EX-0001); it does NOT stop valid commands from a compromised-but-authorized ground system, so it complements rather than replaces the resilience CMs above.
TR52.cm_enisa: ENISA threat-level controls for TR52 are auto-ranked by the tool from enisa_controls.json (standards-traceable to ISO 27001 / NIST CSF / NIS2 / CCSDS); dual-sourced with the SPARTA CMs above.
TR52.impact: Loss of satellite-bus availability; hostile takeover of the platform - malicious command execution and complete lockout of legitimate operators (denial of platform control). CIA: Availability.

conf.note: Graded at the weakest link (R7). The ENISA footprint is directly stated by the primary source - Annex C p.88 gives the affected assets (Ground: TTC Ground [SLE/SDL]; Space: BUS [CDHS/COM]) and CIA=Availability - but the SPARTA side is analyst inference: the IA->EX/LM->IMP stitch and the specific EX-0013.01 choice are reasoned, not sourced. Real-incident analogs (Viasat/AcidRain 2022, hostile TT&C takeover class) support plausibility but not this exact technique stitch, so the record as a whole is Med.

rules: R1 - adversarial (intentional cyber, NAA) -> direct SPARTA mapping
R6 - one ENISA threat -> multiple SPARTA techniques (IA + EX + 2x IMP), evidence per mapping
R8 - each CM bound to a specific stage technique (IA-0007 or EX-0013.01), not the threat category
R9 - affected asset spans Ground + Space -> ONE set-valued record with a bridging sub-chain IA->EX/LM->IMP across the trust boundary
R10 - considered, not triggered: TTP/CM do not differ across the lifecycle set, so L stays a set and the record is not split
evidence: ENISA Space Threat Landscape (2025) Annex C Table 17, p.88 - affected assets + CIA=Availability
SPARTA v3.2 (The Aerospace Corporation) - IA-0007, EX-0013.01, IMP-0002/0003 and their CM mappings (verified against sparta.json)
Viasat KA-SAT / AcidRain (Feb 2022) - real-incident analog for ground-segment compromise -> loss of terminal service (plausibility anchor, not an exact TR52 match)
